text: On today's show, what we're going to do is talk about the differences between working at a big tech FANG company, which I'll talk about that acronym in a second, as well as what it's like to work as a freelancer or consultant.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So what are the two big differences?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So let's hop right into it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So what really is a big tech company?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: When I talk about big tech, I use the word FANG, and that's F-A-A-N-G, which stands for Facebook, Apple, Amazon, Netflix, Google.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: However, that's also more of an encompassing term for big tech.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Meaning that that also includes things like Twitter and Microsoft and LinkedIn and many of the other big tech companies that you're going to see out there in the market as well.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So that's what we're talking about.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: What is it going to be like working as a full-time employee at one of these companies versus working as a freelancer?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, my background is in freelancing and in consulting.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I've been doing that for close to 20 years now, full-time.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So I don't have any exact experience working at a big tech company.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So you're probably wondering who is this guy to give advice on big tech if he's never worked there?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Well, the advice that I'm gonna give today and not even advice is just kind of the anecdotes of what I've learned over the years is from many close friends that have worked at companies such as Facebook, Google, Apple, Instagram, Snapchat, bunch of huge tech companies and I still have friends that work at these companies.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And they've shared a lot of things with me over the years about how things work internally at these big companies.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So that's where a lot of this stuff's gonna come from here.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: How we're gonna structure this video today is gonna be basically two parts.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: We're gonna have the, we're gonna talk about full-time employment at a big tech company.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And we're gonna talk about the pros and the cons of that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And then we're gonna hop over and we're gonna talk about freelancing.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I'm gonna talk about the pros and cons of that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And then we're gonna wrap up at the end with my thought for you on which one you should possibly use.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So let's go ahead and instantly right now hop into the first one, which is gonna be full-time employment at a big tech company.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Okay.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So maybe you've just graduated college or perhaps you've been studying for three or four months through some type of interview tech program, and you're ready to go work at a big tech company or so you think, and you're not really sure if this is the route that you want to go down.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So let's talk about some of the pros of working at a big tech company.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The first and foremost is it's basically a regular job, and that's gonna be way less risky than freelancing or running your own business in general, simply because you're gonna be able to show up, work from nine to five, and you're gonna collect a paycheck on a regular schedule.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That is a humongous benefit, and to be honest, it's something that I even think about often because it eliminates a lot of the stress that I have around perhaps collecting on clients and so forth, which we'll get to in a moment for the freelancer stuff.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But that's going to be the number one biggest thing that you're going to get when you work at a big tech company.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's a solid job.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Most likely these companies aren't going anywhere.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: They're usually on some type of stock index somewhere.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: They have their own stock, their own ticker.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: They're going to be around for a while.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: They're not going to go by fly by night like some startup or they might run out of runway, which is cash in the bank.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Tomorrow they're going to be around for many years and most likely they have very deep pockets.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: which also leads me to it has a wide array of opportunities.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So that's number two.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You have much more opportunity to kind of go different directions at a company.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So if you really want to climb the corporate ladder, perhaps you want to start as a developer, move into senior developer, to team lead, then maybe into management, working in a big tech company is going to give you that option because as over time, you can start moving in that direction pretty easily because all those options are available.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The bigger the company, the more positions are available because smaller managerial units exist so the company can operate effectively.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And as you're staying there and moving up, you can kind of decide where you want to go.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Would you like to stay in the technical track?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Do you want to go into more managerial track?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Perhaps you want to make even a sideways move and go over into sales or you want to go into R&D and engineering or something.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There's a bunch of different things you can do in big tech.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So you kind of have a bunch of options while also still keeping
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: your kind of firm foundational base of having full-time employment, yet not really getting too risky, but having a few options out there.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Another benefit of working at a full-time big tech company is your benefits are fantastic usually.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Your health, dental, life, disability, any of your insurances that you have are usually gonna be pretty good insurance.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And a lot of times it's almost covered 100%.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Again, this really depends on the company that you're working for, but you really find some really good insurance.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: which also kind of leads into the additional part of the benefit package of you're most likely going to get some level of stock options.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This can be quite a huge amount of money compared to what you're used to getting.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I've seen anywhere from a few hundred thousand dollars to a couple million dollars in stock options that are offered to a full-time employees at a big tech company.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The thing though is you just have to be aware of how these stocks vest, which usually means over a period of time, you're gonna get access to those stocks until they're actually yours.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Typically, that's going to be between two to five years.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I think the average is anywhere between three to four of where you like will fully vest.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So that does take time.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: However, it can offer you a significant amount of additional income if you decide to go with a big tech company because those benefits are so big and enormous.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Another thing is, if you really don't wanna be pushed really that hard, perhaps you really love your family environment, you are participating in your church, you have some extracurricular activities, you're coaching teams, and you really just wanna enjoy your eight hours at work and then go live your life, working at a big tech company is gonna provide you with some of that freedom.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, of course, you're gonna have your long hours at any company such as a big tech company, but...
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: By actually working at a big tech company, you can actually have the opportunity to perhaps step away and do something outside of work that isn't directly related to tech.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So as some people would say, if you just kind of want to skate by for a little while, you can kind of do that at a big tech company.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I don't recommend that you do it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: However, it is something that a lot of people will do because it's a full time job.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And as the seasons ebb and flow, some people become more productive and less productive and things kind of go with the flow.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And it's easier to do that when you're a full time employee at a big tech company.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And lastly, one of the big things you're really going to get a benefit from is that you're going to work on large scale problems that you probably will have never experienced anywhere else in your career.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Big tech companies have a level of scale that is just astronomical.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Most companies will never even come close to the level of scale than which Facebook and Google and all these big tech companies have simply because of the amount of traffic that they have.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: They're going to find weird edge cases on wall with types of weird platforms and so forth.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And you're going to have to solve all of these problems that you would have never had to solve before.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So it is actually an opportunity to kind of learn very deep, specialized knowledge in one particular area.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So that can be seen as a benefit as well.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So that kind of wraps it up for the pros of working at a big tech company.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I'm sure there's more, but that's kind of the high level that I wanted to talk about.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So now let's talk about the cons of working for a big tech company.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Number one, corporate politics.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's gonna be your biggest problem, in my opinion, of working at a big tech company.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, a lot of big tech companies are gonna say, well, we actually are kind of like a startup, you see, because we have a startup-like culture, but when it comes down to it, it's all internal corporate politics at a base level.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There's gonna be a lot of backstabbing going on.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There's gonna be a lot of people trying to climb to the top at whatever cost necessary.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There's gonna be a lot of trash talking.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It is what it is.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Nothing's going to be perfect.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I have yet to see a perfect company, yet these things exist much more in a big tech company simply because of all the corporate politics and everything that happens inside of a big tech company.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: All right.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The next one is also a really big one.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And this is actually the reason why I don't really work at big tech companies is that they essentially own you and almost everything that you create.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: certain big tech companies will lock you into a contract stating that anything that you create outside of your full time employment, the company has rights to as well.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, I know some companies have relaxed their terms on this, but it's still very common, especially if the technology is very similar to what you're working on at your full time job.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So what that means is you can't go create side projects.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You can't maybe have outside clients.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Some of these companies do allow you to do this.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I understand that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But for the most part, you're gonna have to get some level of approval from some level VP to go out and work on any side projects, to show up on podcasts, perhaps even to write blog posts.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And you especially have to be very careful of what you're doing on any types of social media.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But the big ones really are any of your side projects.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If you wanna start a side hustle or a side business,
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You might not be able to do that when you're working at a big tech company, simply because the company might own rights to anything that you work at and work on outside of your work hours because of the work agreement that you signed with them.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So you have to check your work agreement.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: but a lot of these companies do own a lot of the things that you do.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Again, I know there's stipulations and I know there's things have changed, but that does exist and it's something that you do need to be aware of.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Another con is that every move you have is gonna be scrutinized.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: As I mentioned, anything about social media, anytime you post anything on social media, it can be seen as something negative against the company.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If you disagree,
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: with a direction that a company is moving in either a political front or even on a front that you don't agree with on some other type of public stance, that is something that you can't control.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And so that can be a real opposition to maybe your core beliefs and values.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If you wanna actually decide to stay with that company, are you...
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: implicitly accepting what they're doing and accepting it and moving it forward?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Or are you going to fight back?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And if you fight back with it, that could end your employment with the company, which could be a bad thing.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So it's something that you do have to think about.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Mainly though, is that every move that you have is going to be scrutinized.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It could be positive or it could be negative.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Again, that's something you have to weigh each time you do something.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It could be a blog post, social media post, anything.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, earlier I referenced in the pro section that you get to work on very challenging problems such as really weird edge cases because of the scale of these companies.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's a very valid point.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: However, there's also the situation and this is most of the time the situation is you're going to be working on something that you have absolutely no interest on.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You're going to be put on a team and your focus is going to be on this component or this part of the screen.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Don, you're going to be working on the about screen and you're going to show all of the version numbers for all the open source libraries we have and we want those to animate in.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: sounds super boring to me, but there's someone who has to do that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And there's someone who has to fix certain screens on a certain level device that's only available in a third world country.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And it's due to this one thing that has to get fixed.

text: Now I'm not saying it shouldn't be fixed, but I'm saying is that you may not be interested in the work that you're doing.

text: So you really don't get to choose what you want to work on.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Sure, you might be able to transfer into a department to work on something else.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But again, you're kind of just a worker bee in the entire system.

text: And so you're gonna be assigned to do something, whether you like it or not, which can be a real bummer.

text: Now that kind of leads us to the next topic of bosses.

text: What if you don't like your boss or do you do like your boss?

text: Well, you're kind of stuck just like any full-time job is.

text: You're gonna be stuck working for that boss regardless if you like them or not until you can transfer.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So again, kind of a bummer.

text: Next is limited holiday time.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, if a company tells you that they have unlimited holiday time, that's an absolute lie.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That does not exist.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: good luck with that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's not gonna happen.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So one of the things you need to realize is that that's completely out of the picture and it's often better to have a company that sets a certain number of vacation days you get per year.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That could be two weeks or four weeks or whatever you negotiate.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But again, your holiday time is limited.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If you decide for whatever reason you have, say you have $2 million in the bank and you wanna go take off two or three months,
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: and you don't have that vacation time, if you can't get that approved somehow, you have to go to work.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So essentially, you're still working for Le Mans, as they say, and you still have to show up and do the work, which can kind of be a tremendous downfall, especially if you have been saving properly and you wanna do something different from your life than working full time.

text: Again, we'll get to the freelancing thing, which may be more interesting to you if that's your case, but that's something that needs to be seen as a possible con, that you don't have full control of your holiday time.

text: And lastly, this is one that's really important to me.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This is commuting.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Commuting is a humongous negative for working for big tech companies.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I've been offered jobs from almost all of the big tech companies at one point in time in my career, and all of them wanted me to either move to San Francisco, Los Angeles, London, New York, Singapore, or various other different countries throughout the world.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: and they wanted me to work on site.

text: Now, a lot of this has changed with the post pandemic.

text: However, I'm already starting to see some companies requiring their employees to come back on site.

text: I'm not saying there's no benefit to working on site with your peers.

text: There is a lot of benefit to that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: However, I've been working remotely for over 15 years now.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And if you use the right communication protocol, which everything is gonna be autonomous at that point in time, and you build your communication mechanism around that, the entire team can be very effective anywhere in the world.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And there's many companies that have done this with success.

speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Number one, freedom.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: By far, you are the master of your own destiny.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You get to control everything that you do going forward.

text: Okay, that's cool.

text: It's actually a positive with freelancing, meaning that you can take as much time as you want off, of course, as long as you can afford it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You really don't have a boss you report to.

text: You can control your income as much as you want, again, up to an hourly rate.

text: There's a lot of the tax code that's written for businesses, so it can work in your benefit.

text: You just have to talk to an accountant to figure out how to properly structure your own freelancing business, either an S Corp or an LLC, so that you can maximize your tax benefits at the end of the year, so you don't really get raked over the coals and paying a ton of tremendous tax at the end of the year.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: One of the cons of working at a full-time company is you can't have any side projects or any side hustles going on.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: With freelancing, you can build a side business or a couple of them if you want.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You can have multiple clients going at once.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You could have multiple clients.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Perhaps if you work in the fitness space, you can have multiple fitness clients.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, as long as you're not sharing any of the proprietary information across them and there's no non-competes between the contracts, you're fine.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If you have maybe clients in real estate and clients in healthcare, you can work on both of those.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Your options really open up is what you can do and you can build your own business and no one's gonna say, hey, we own that because you were working under our umbrella during that time.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's all stipulated in your contract that none of that applies.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And very similarly, no one can tell you what you can or can't work on.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Do you want to record a podcast?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Great.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Do you want to do a blog?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Cool.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You want to do some videos?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Awesome.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The options are up to you to what you want to go out there and do.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Ultimately, all of the pros here point to endless freedom for you.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's going to open up a wide array for you to do whatever you want and give you pure autonomy in how you operate your life, which to me is priceless.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Okay, let's be serious now.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There's some freelancing cons here.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I'm not gonna lie.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Freelancing is stressful.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You live in what's known as a feast or famine life cycle.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There's gonna be times when you have a lot of clients.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There's more work than you know what to do with.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And then six months later, there's nothing.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's a famine.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's a desert.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You have nothing.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And your full-time job is then finding a client.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Sometimes you have to drop your rates just so you can get some more money coming in the door.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Hopefully you've been billing high enough and if you haven't, you can kind of be in a bind.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If you're not sure of what you should be billing and that's a confusing topic for you, check out this card right here and it'll show you everything you need to know about billing the right amount.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But assuming you're billing the right amount, you should have enough saved up so you can kind of weather these feast and famine cycles.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: However, that doesn't take away from the stress that's there.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Furthermore, being your own freelancer and being self-employed is much more work than a full-time employee.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You are going to be working your eight hours for your client.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You also have administrative work to do.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You still need to write your blog posts.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You still need to be getting out there and marketing yourself so you can find your next client.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You need to be doing sales for your next client.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So you have more than eight hour days going on.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Very often I see freelancers and consultants work anywhere from 40 to 60 hours a week.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This is until they start building high enough where they can drop their hours back to maybe 25, 35 hours a week and use those extra hours that normally would take up the 40 hours.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: to start building and working on the additional things that I just mentioned.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So again, this really depends on how you're managing your time, your clients, and your income.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So it's something you have to be very careful of, but it is definitely more work than a full-time job, and it's something that you're always thinking about.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Well, that light turned off in the background.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Next, your paycheck is not guaranteed.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This one is a really hard pill to swallow.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: When you're used to getting paid every other week, twice a month, whatever your schedule is, and then you start freelancing, your paychecks are never regular.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You might get paid on time from a client one month, and then the next month they just, oops, forgot to pay you.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And then you're waiting six to eight weeks before you've been paid.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This can be insanely stressful.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: very often I find that I have to collect on about 85 to 90% of my clients.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And what that means is, as soon as the payment date is due, it's 30 days after I've sent my payment, that's a net 30 payment, I still haven't received payment.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I then have to get on the phone or an email, ask them, hey, where's the payment?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And it's usually, oh, oops, we forgot, sorry.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Yeah, it's going out today.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And then that goes out through the U.S. mail, which unfortunately things get lost in the mail and that has happened to me as well.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So you do have to collect on your clients, which can be very stressful.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This then leads into the legality portion of it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You have to manage your legal components correctly.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's also an additional stress.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You have to have your contract set up and written by a good lawyer.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You need to have good accountants.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And the contract is really key here.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Because getting back to getting paid from a client, if they don't pay you and you have a contract with them that they're supposed to pay you, now you can get your lawyer involved to have them legally contact the company and start talking about a lawsuit if they don't pay you because it's stipulated in the contract.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And most likely you're going to win that lawsuit if your contract is solid.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, this is something I actually have experience with.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I've had to do this.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Clients did not pay me.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I had to get my lawyer involved and...
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Almost got into a legal battle and eventually I got paid.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Unfortunately, it was three or four months after the due date.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So I had to front all the money myself to pay my subcontractors that I had hired under me.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So they were paid and I kind of had to skim by to get through those few months until I was able to get paid again.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So again, this goes back to the stress.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's much more stressful than a regular full-time job.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Next, we have insurance.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There's no guaranteed insurance for you.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You're not going to get health insurance, dental insurance or anything like that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You have to buy your own insurance.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's going to be expensive and it's going to be time consuming to find.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And it's just kind of how it goes.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Again, you're not going to get anything like 401k, any retirement plans, stock options.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This is why it's crucial for you to be building the correct amount.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If you can build the right amount, you can save for all these things and make just as much, if not more than you would have when you're working a full time employee.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: However, it's just gonna take more work and you have to be more disciplined and organized about it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Otherwise, you could end up upside down, which is definitely not what you want.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Next, you gotta pay your own taxes.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This is a big one.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You are gonna get a check that's very large from clients, five-figure checks very often, and you have to make sure to realize that all that money's not yours.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: you have to take a certain percentage of that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Basically about 30 to 40% of that is the government.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And that's a very hard thing to do at first.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Let's just use a round number.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You get $10,000 check.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's a lot of money.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You get to put it in your bank account and you see that number in your bank account like, wow, I got a lot of money.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And then you have to think, well, now the government gets 30 to 40% of that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I just have to save that to the side.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I just have to give it away.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's something very hard and difficult for a lot of people to grasp, but it's something that you have to do.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Otherwise you'll get in hot water with the IRS and you'll end up on a payment plan or they'll come after you for back taxes, which is never a good thing.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So you want to make sure that you're paying your taxes, have a good accountant to make sure that you're paying your quarterlies on time and to make sure that you're filing your state and federal taxes accordingly.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Keeping your skills sharp is one of the other things you have to also do that's a con inside of freelancing.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, you have to do this anyway when you're a full-time employee, but when you're a full-time employee, a lot of times there's on-the-job training.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And as a freelancer, you don't get that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You have to do that on your own free time.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There's simply no time for you to do it on the job.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Yes, you're going to learn certain things on the job, but if a brand new technology comes out...
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: you're probably not going to get paid to implement it if you don't even know it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So you're probably going to have to go learn it on your own.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So that's going to be all on your own dime, which can be pretty expensive and very time consuming.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And lastly, there's no defined career path for a freelancer.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I started freelancing and consulting 15, 20 years ago.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: In fact, if we were really to count my original time when I started freelancing and consulting was in the late 90s.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So that was part time.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So upwards of 20 plus years of freelancing and consulting and there's no career path.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Where do you go?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You're your own boss.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You're already at the top.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So what do you do?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Do you want to open an agency?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You could.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Do you want to build a product and sell a product as software?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You could do that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Then that's not freelancing anymore.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Do you want to be a manager?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But what are you a manager of?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So there's real no defined career path.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: You have to really understand what you're going for in your life.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If you want to become a manager somewhere where then maybe freelancing isn't for you.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But if you're mainly your goal is to...
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: create income and generate assets from that income.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Perhaps you wanna buy real estate.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Perhaps you wanna get into some other types of assets and you're gonna use freelancing as an income source to do that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Well, then you could very well do that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And perhaps you put a timetable of 10 years on that and say, in 10 years, I wanna have this number of assets so I can just basically stop freelancing.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The freelancing will be a great opportunity for you to do that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But if you're looking to move up and be a freelancer for 15 or 20 years and then walk in and be a VP or a CTO of a company,
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's very unlikely to happen unless you happen to fall into a weird situation where a client needs a CTO or a VP and they offer the job to you, which does happen.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It has happened to me, but I would never bet on those situations occurring.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So there is no defined career path for freelancing and consulting.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So it's something you have to keep in mind.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And maybe this ends up only being something temporary for you so you can see what type of projects and what types of industries you wanna get into.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's very often the case that a lot of people will do freelancing and consulting for five years and then go full-time at a company somewhere else after they've determined what they like and don't like.

text: There's the pros, there's the cons of both full-time employment as well as freelancing.

text: You need to decide what's best for you.

text: Everybody is different.

text: I have mentored multiple people into freelancing and helped them succeed in freelancing and they have enjoyed it.

text: I've also mentored many people into freelancing who did not enjoy it and went back to full-time employment.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: They liked the consistency of the eight to five job and getting off and coming home and not thinking about work anymore.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Everybody's wired differently.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Do you want the easy job where you hang out nine to five, but you're kind of out of control and everything else?

text: Yeah, maybe you get some stock options.

text: Or do you want more freedom and autonomy in your life, but it comes with a lot more work and a lot more stress?

text: and perhaps makes you a lot more short-term, but doesn't give you a career path.

text: There's benefits to both.
